content:
<system_instruction>
You are working inside Conductor, a Mac app that lets the user run many coding agents in parallel.
Your work should take place in the /Users/fcamblor/conductor/workspaces/mac-ai-trackers/chennai directory (unless otherwise directed), which has been set up for you to work in.
Each workspace has a .context directory (gitignored) where you can save files to collaborate with other agents.
The target branch for this workspace is origin/main. Use this for actions like diffing (`git diff origin/main...`) or creating PRs (`gh pr create --base main`).

Do not rename the current branch unless the user explicitly tells you to do so.

By default, the user will only see the last message that you send before stopping. Include all essential information in the last message. The intermediate messages will be collapsed and accessible by the user but not displayed by default.

If the user asks you to work on several unrelated tasks, you can suggest they start new workspaces.
Sometimes the user might send you a message they meant to send in a different workspace or a different chat. If something doesn't make sense in the context of your work, just ask. 
If the user asks for help with Conductor, you can ask them to go to "Help -> Send Feedback" to get in touch with our team.
</system_instruction>

## context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

## plugin:serena:serena
CRITICAL: Before starting to work on a coding task, call the `initial_instructions` tool to read the 'Serena Instructions Manual'.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 â†’ 4.6, 4.6 â†’ 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.
- quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- archon: Use when: User wants to run Archon workflows, CREATE workflows or commands,
set up Archon, or manage Archon configuration.
Triggers (run): "use archon to", "run archon", "archon workflow", "use archon for",
          "have archon", "let archon", "ask archon to".
Triggers (create): "create a workflow", "write a workflow", "make a command",
          "author a workflow", "new workflow", "new command", "archon workflow yaml".
Triggers (setup): "set up archon", "install archon", "how to use archon",
          "configure archon", "archon setup", "get started with archon".
Triggers (config): "change my archon config", "modify archon config", "archon config",
          "change archon settings", "update my config", "help me change my config",
          "edit archon config", "archon configuration".
Triggers (init): "initialize archon", "set up .archon", "archon init", "add archon to repo".
Capability: Runs AI workflows in isolated git worktrees for parallel development.
Also: Creates and manages workflow YAML files, command files, and configuration.
NOT for: Direct Claude Code work - only for delegating to Archon CLI.
- shape: Plan the UX and UI for a feature before writing code. Runs a structured discovery interview, then produces a design brief that guides implementation. Use during the planning phase to establish design direction, constraints, and strategy before any code is written.
- optimize: Diagnoses and fixes UI performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Use when the user mentions slow, laggy, janky, performance, bundle size, load time, or wants a faster, smoother experience.
- adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.
- impeccable: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics. Use when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications, or when any design skill requires project context. Call with 'craft' for shape-then-build, 'teach' for design context setup, or 'extract' to pull reusable components and tokens into the design system.
- clarify: Improve unclear UX copy, error messages, microcopy, labels, and instructions to make interfaces easier to understand. Use when the user mentions confusing text, unclear labels, bad error messages, hard-to-follow instructions, or wanting better UX writing.
- layout: Improve layout, spacing, and visual rhythm. Fixes monotonous grids, inconsistent spacing, and weak visual hierarchy. Use when the user mentions layout feeling off, spacing issues, visual hierarchy, crowded UI, alignment problems, or wanting better composition.
- dbg: Debug applications using the dbg CLI debugger. Supports Node.js (V8/CDP), Bun (WebKit/JSC), and native code via LLDB (DAP). Use when: (1) investigating runtime bugs by stepping through code, (2) inspecting variable values at specific execution points, (3) setting breakpoints and conditional breakpoints, (4) evaluating expressions in a paused context, (5) hot-patching code without restarting (JS/TS), (6) debugging test failures by attaching to a running process, (7) debugging C/C++/Rust/Swift with LLDB, (8) any task where understanding runtime behavior requires a debugger. Triggers: "debug this", "set a breakpoint", "step through", "inspect variables", "why is this value wrong", "trace execution", "attach debugger", "runtime error", "segfault", "core dump".
- distill: Strip designs to their essence by removing unnecessary complexity. Great design is simple, powerful, and clean. Use when the user asks to simplify, declutter, reduce noise, remove elements, or make a UI cleaner and more focused.
- delight: Add moments of joy, personality, and unexpected touches that make interfaces memorable and enjoyable to use. Elevates functional to delightful. Use when the user asks to add polish, personality, animations, micro-interactions, delight, or make an interface feel fun or memorable.
- audit: Run technical quality checks across accessibility, performance, theming, responsive design, and anti-patterns. Generates a scored report with P0-P3 severity ratings and actionable plan. Use when the user wants an accessibility check, performance audit, or technical quality review.
- polish: Performs a final quality pass fixing alignment, spacing, consistency, and micro-detail issues before shipping. Use when the user mentions polish, finishing touches, pre-launch review, something looks off, or wants to go from good to great.
- bolder: Amplify safe or boring designs to make them more visually interesting and stimulating. Increases impact while maintaining usability. Use when the user says the design looks bland, generic, too safe, lacks personality, or wants more visual impact and character.
- typeset: Improves typography by fixing font choices, hierarchy, sizing, weight, and readability so text feels intentional. Use when the user mentions fonts, type, readability, text hierarchy, sizing looks off, or wants more polished, intentional typography.
- critique: Evaluate design from a UX perspective, assessing visual hierarchy, information architecture, emotional resonance, cognitive load, and overall quality with quantitative scoring, persona-based testing, automated anti-pattern detection, and actionable feedback. Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.
- colorize: Add strategic color to features that are too monochromatic or lack visual interest, making interfaces more engaging and expressive. Use when the user mentions the design looking gray, dull, lacking warmth, needing more color, or wanting a more vibrant or expressive palette.
- overdrive: Pushes interfaces past conventional limits with technically ambitious implementations â€” shaders, spring physics, scroll-driven reveals, 60fps animations. Use when the user wants to wow, impress, go all-out, or make something that feels extraordinary.
- tech-debt: Create a new technical debt entry in the tech-debt/ directory. Use when identifying technical debt to formalize, track, or document for future resolution.
- improve-ai-harness: Analyze review feedbacks or development friction points and suggest improvements to the AI harness (rules, CLAUDE.md, docs, tooling). Use when asked to improve AI rules, after a code review with issues, or to strengthen the AI configuration.
- roadmap-feature: Create a new epic entry in the roadmap/ directory. Use when the user wants to plan a new feature, add an item to the roadmap, or formalize an epic for future implementation.
- session-save: Save the current session state â€” decisions, modified files, current status, and next steps â€” to a handoff file for later resume.
- learn-from-mistakes: Now that you learnt some new stuff, write detailed specs and reduce ambiguity before handing work...
- commit: Commit les changements du workspace git avec guidelines opinionated (conventional commits dĂ©tectĂ©s, langue dĂ©tectĂ©e, fixup automatique)
- pr-context: Load the full context of a GitHub Pull Request so the user can ask questions about it.
- log-4sh-time:log-4sh-time: Interactive workflow to log work time in 4track with semantic task matching. View aggregated work statistics by project, perimeter, and task. Use this skill when the user wants to create a worklog, log hours, track time, record work activities, or analyze time spent on tasks.
- typescript-standards:enforce-ts-standards: Enforce type-safe TypeScript patterns with Zod validation, pattern matching, and discriminated unions to prevent runtime errors. Use when writing TypeScript code, reviewing for type safety, designing data validation flows, or refactoring to eliminate impossible states.
- oss-security-audit:audit-oss-package: Security audit of a third-party package (npm, pip, homebrew, cargo, gemâ€¦) before
using it. Fetches its source code, scans for malicious or suspicious patterns
(outbound network calls, telemetry, eval/exec, install hooks, opaque file writes,
supply-chain risks), then recursively audits same-author dependencies found in the
package manifest.
Use when the user says "is this package safe", "audit this dependency",
"check this npm/pip/brew package", "can I trust this library", or asks
whether a package is trustworthy before adding it to a project.
- impeccable:animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.
- impeccable:layout: Improve layout, spacing, and visual rhythm. Fixes monotonous grids, inconsistent spacing, and weak visual hierarchy. Use when the user mentions layout feeling off, spacing issues, visual hierarchy, crowded UI, alignment problems, or wanting better composition.
- impeccable:quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- impeccable:adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.
- impeccable:clarify: Improve unclear UX copy, error messages, microcopy, labels, and instructions to make interfaces easier to understand. Use when the user mentions confusing text, unclear labels, bad error messages, hard-to-follow instructions, or wanting better UX writing.
- impeccable:impeccable: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics. Use when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications, or when any design skill requires project context. Call with 'craft' for shape-then-build, 'teach' for design context setup, or 'extract' to pull reusable components and tokens into the design system.
- impeccable:colorize: Add strategic color to features that are too monochromatic or lack visual interest, making interfaces more engaging and expressive. Use when the user mentions the design looking gray, dull, lacking warmth, needing more color, or wanting a more vibrant or expressive palette.
- impeccable:optimize: Diagnoses and fixes UI performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Use when the user mentions slow, laggy, janky, performance, bundle size, load time, or wants a faster, smoother experience.
- impeccable:distill: Strip designs to their essence by removing unnecessary complexity. Great design is simple, powerful, and clean. Use when the user asks to simplify, declutter, reduce noise, remove elements, or make a UI cleaner and more focused.
- impeccable:typeset: Improves typography by fixing font choices, hierarchy, sizing, weight, and readability so text feels intentional. Use when the user mentions fonts, type, readability, text hierarchy, sizing looks off, or wants more polished, intentional typography.
- impeccable:harden: Make interfaces production-ready: error handling, empty states, onboarding flows, i18n, text overflow, and edge case management. Use when the user asks to harden, make production-ready, handle edge cases, add error states, design empty states, improve onboarding, or fix overflow and i18n issues.
- impeccable:overdrive: Pushes interfaces past conventional limits with technically ambitious implementations â€” shaders, spring physics, scroll-driven reveals, 60fps animations. Use when the user wants to wow, impress, go all-out, or make something that feels extraordinary.
- impeccable:shape: Plan the UX and UI for a feature before writing code. Runs a structured discovery interview, then produces a design brief that guides implementation. Use during the planning phase to establish design direction, constraints, and strategy before any code is written.
- impeccable:bolder: Amplify safe or boring designs to make them more visually interesting and stimulating. Increases impact while maintaining usability. Use when the user says the design looks bland, generic, too safe, lacks personality, or wants more visual impact and character.
- impeccable:polish: Performs a final quality pass fixing alignment, spacing, consistency, and micro-detail issues before shipping. Use when the user mentions polish, finishing touches, pre-launch review, something looks off, or wants to go from good to great.
- impeccable:critique: Evaluate design from a UX perspective, assessing visual hierarchy, information architecture, emotional resonance, cognitive load, and overall quality with quantitative scoring, persona-based testing, automated anti-pattern detection, and actionable feedback. Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.
- impeccable:audit: Run technical quality checks across accessibility, performance, theming, responsive design, and anti-patterns. Generates a scored report with P0-P3 severity ratings and actionable plan. Use when the user wants an accessibility check, performance audit, or technical quality review.
- impeccable:delight: Add moments of joy, personality, and unexpected touches that make interfaces memorable and enjoyable to use. Elevates functional to delightful. Use when the user asks to add polish, personality, animations, micro-interactions, delight, or make an interface feel fun or memorable.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking:
The crash happens in the binding's getterâ€”when a chart gets deleted, the ForEach updates but the binding still holds the old index value, and since the closure captures `index` by value, SwiftUI calls the getter later with an out-of-bounds index that the guard check at line 160 never catches.

The real solution is to look up the configuration by its ID instead of relying on a captured index, so the binding dynamically finds the correct item even if the array shifts around during deletion.

content.text:
Le crash est dans `ChartConfigurationCard.configurationBinding` Ă  la ligne 162. Le problĂ¨me est classique avec SwiftUI : lors de la suppression, le `Binding` capture `index` mais la closure `get` est rĂ©-Ă©valuĂ©e par SwiftUI pendant la transaction de suppression, Ă  un moment oĂą l'index est devenu hors-bornes (la card est encore vivante avec l'ancien index alors que le tableau a dĂ©jĂ  Ă©tĂ© rĂ©duit). Le `guard` au-dessus du `Binding(...)` ne protĂ¨ge que la crĂ©ation, pas les invocations futures du getter/setter.

La correction robuste : indexer par `id` (et non par position) dans le binding lui-mĂŞme, pour que la rĂ©solution soit toujours faite Ă  partir de l'Ă©tat courant.

content.thinking: I'm refactoring `ChartConfigurationCard` to pass the configuration's `id` instead of relying on `index`, then resolving the index dynamically within the binding closures on each callâ€”this keeps moves working since they don't destroy the view between updates.

content.text: Je rends le binding rĂ©silient en rĂ©solvant l'index via l'`id` Ă  chaque accĂ¨s (au lieu de capturer un index figĂ© qui peut devenir invalide pendant la transaction de suppression).

content.thinking:
The `body` uses `if let configurationBinding` to handle deletion gracefully â€” when the configuration is removed, the binding returns nil and the body stays empty, which is exactly what we want. The getter's fallback to a fresh ChartConfiguration shouldn't occur given the guard, but it's there for type safety. During each render where the item exists, that closure should locate the binding correctly.

There's a potential edge case where SwiftUI reads the binding after deletion, but even then the fallback returns an empty ChartConfiguration, which is harmless since the body would re-evaluate `configurationBinding` as nil on the next render and disappear. Let me build this to verify it works.

content.text:
Build OK (les diagnostics SourceKit sont prĂ©-existants et non liĂ©s Ă  ma modification â€” `@Bindable var store: UsageStore` Ă©tait dĂ©jĂ  sur ces 4 lignes ; le `swift build` rĂ©ussit).

## RĂ©sumĂ© du fix

**Cause du crash** (`ChartSettingsView.swift:162`) : le `Binding` capturait l'`index` de la card par valeur. Pendant la transaction SwiftUI de suppression, le getter du binding est rĂ©-Ă©valuĂ© *aprĂ¨s* que `chartConfigurations.removeAll { ... }` ait rĂ©duit le tableau, mais *avant* que la card soit retirĂ©e de l'arbre de vues â€” d'oĂą le `Array._checkSubscript` fatal. Le `guard preferences.chartConfigurations.indices.contains(index)` ne protĂ©geait que la crĂ©ation du binding, pas ses appels ultĂ©rieurs.

**Correction** :
- `ChartConfigurationCard` reĂ§oit maintenant `configurationID: UUID` (au lieu de `index: Int`).
- Le getter/setter du `Binding` re-rĂ©solvent l'index par `id` Ă  chaque appel via `firstIndex(where:)` / `first(where:)`. Ainsi, mĂŞme si un autre Ă©lĂ©ment a Ă©tĂ© supprimĂ© entre-temps, l'accĂ¨s reste cohĂ©rent.
- Le callback `onRequestDelete` capture directement `configuration.id` depuis le `ForEach` (plus de `chartConfigurations[index]` post-deletion).
- Les opĂ©rations de move continuent d'utiliser l'`index` (pas de problĂ¨me de rĂ©entrance pendant un dĂ©placement).

content.text:
# Smart commit

Tu dois committer les changements git en cours dans le repo courant en suivant les guidelines ci-dessous.

## Contexte utilisateur

Intent fourni par l'utilisateur (peut ĂŞtre vide) : ``

## Ă‰tapes

### 1. Analyser l'Ă©tat du repo

Lance en parallĂ¨le :
- `git status` (sans `-uall`)
- `git diff --staged` et `git diff` pour voir les changements
- `git log -n 5 --pretty=format:'%h %s%n%b%n---'` pour voir les 4-5 derniers commits

### 2. DĂ©tecter les conventions du projet

Ă€ partir des 4-5 derniers commits :
- **Conventional commits ?** Si la majoritĂ© suit le format `type(scope): message` (feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, build, ci...) â†’ utilise ce format. Sinon â†’ format libre.
- **Langue ?** DĂ©tecte la langue dominante des messages (franĂ§ais, anglais, autre) â†’ utilise la mĂŞme langue. En cas de doute, franĂ§ais.
- **Style ?** ImpĂ©ratif vs descriptif, majuscule initiale ou non, prĂ©sence de scope, etc. â†’ imite.

### 3. DĂ©tecter un Ă©ventuel fixup

Si l'intent utilisateur mentionne un correctif (mots-clĂ©s : "fix", "corrige", "rĂ©pare", "bug", "issue", "problĂ¨me", "rĂ©gression", "broken"...) :

1. Identifie les fichiers modifiĂ©s (`git diff --name-only HEAD` + fichiers staged).
2. Pour chaque fichier, regarde `git log -n 10 --oneline -- <fichier>` pour voir quels commits rĂ©cents l'ont touchĂ©.
3. Si **un seul commit rĂ©cent** (dans les ~5 derniers, non encore pushĂ© sur main/master partagĂ© â€” vĂ©rifie avec `git log @{u}..HEAD` si upstream) est clairement Ă  l'origine du code cassĂ©, propose un **`git commit --fixup=<sha>`** au lieu d'un commit classique.
4. Si ambigu ou si le commit date d'avant `@{u}` (dĂ©jĂ  pushĂ© en amont partagĂ©), reste sur un commit classique.

Ne fais **pas** de rebase autosquash automatiquement â€” laisse ce soin Ă  l'utilisateur.

### 4. RĂ©diger le message

RĂ¨gles impĂ©ratives :
- **Le message explique le POURQUOI**, pas le QUOI. Le diff dit dĂ©jĂ  ce qui change. Le message dit pourquoi ce changement existe (motivation, contrainte, problĂ¨me rĂ©solu, contexte mĂ©tier).
- Sujet court (â‰¤72 caractĂ¨res), en suivant la convention dĂ©tectĂ©e.
- Si le pourquoi ne tient pas dans le sujet, ajoute un corps (ligne vide + explication). Sinon, pas de corps inutile.
- Pas de name-dropping de fichiers/fonctions dans le sujet â€” le diff suffit.
- Pas de phrases vides genre Â« Mise Ă  jour du code Â» ou Â« Divers fixes Â».

Si l'intent utilisateur est vide ou trop vague pour dĂ©duire le pourquoi, et que le diff ne le rend pas Ă©vident, **demande Ă  l'utilisateur** avant de committer plutĂ´t que d'inventer.

### 5. Staging et commit

- Si rien n'est staged, stage les fichiers pertinents **par nom** (jamais `git add -A` ni `git add .` pour Ă©viter d'embarquer secrets/binaires).
- Ignore les fichiers suspects (`.env`, `credentials*`, clĂ©s privĂ©es, dumps volumineux). PrĂ©viens l'utilisateur si tu en vois.
- CrĂ©e le commit via HEREDOC pour prĂ©server le formatage multiligne.
- **Ne jamais** utiliser `--no-verify`, `--amend` (sauf demande explicite), ni `--no-gpg-sign`.
- Si un hook pre-commit Ă©choue : diagnostique la cause, corrige, re-stage, et crĂ©e un **nouveau** commit (pas d'amend).

### 6. VĂ©rification finale

AprĂ¨s commit, lance `git status` et `git log -1` pour confirmer. Reporte briĂ¨vement : sha court + sujet + (fixup le cas Ă©chĂ©ant).

## Garde-fous

- Ne push jamais.
- Ne crĂ©e pas de commit vide.
- Ne touche pas Ă  `git config`.
- Si plusieurs "unitĂ©s logiques" distinctes sont mĂ©langĂ©es dans le diff, prĂ©viens l'utilisateur et propose de splitter plutĂ´t que de tout entasser. Tu peux utiliser `git stash` (avec `-u`/`--keep-index` si pertinent) pour isoler des sous-ensembles â€” mais cette commande n'est **pas** auto-approuvĂ©e, l'utilisateur devra confirmer Ă  chaque usage : ne l'utilise que si c'est vraiment nĂ©cessaire.

1. Analyser l'Ă©tat du repo
2. DĂ©tecter les conventions du projet
3. DĂ©tecter un Ă©ventuel fixup
4. RĂ©diger le message
5. Staging et commit
6. VĂ©rification finale

content.content:
3c2d4d1 fix(launch-at-login): unify state check and make registration idempotent
Repeated setEnabled(true) calls from swift run were stacking duplicate legacy
login items because SMAppService.mainApp.register() â€” when called from a
non-bundled binary â€” has no idempotence and appends rather than merges.

Now isEnabled checks both BTM (modern SMAppService entry) and legacy System
Events login items, so the idempotence guard reliably detects the true state
whether running from .app bundle or swift run. Legacy items are queried via
AppleScript (requires Automation permission, gracefully falls back if denied).

Also add NSAppleEventsUsageDescription to Info.plist so macOS permits the
Automation query.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: a2737be95386

---
839ef3c Fix Codex token expiry false positives

---
d5fe9fd refactor(chart): sliding window offset for history navigation
Replace absolute date storage (historyWindowEndDate) with an offset
integer to ensure windows always slide forward with time. Each 60s tick
now recalculates referenceDate = Date() - (offset Ă— interval), making
navigation-history windows glide naturally. Offset resets to 0 on window
size changes.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: 9d7acd2ebaa4

---
9336a0a fix(chart): clamp anchor point timestamps to window boundaries
Chart lines previously extended outside the visible x-domain when
displaying pre-window and post-window anchor points. Anchor points now
have their timestamps clamped to startDate (left edge) and now (right
edge), so chart lines remain visually contained within the window's
fixed domain.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: 2597abdce6f8

---
c883dc7 fix(chart): display date and time in hover legend for all time windows
The chart hover legend now consistently shows both date and time
(e.g. "Apr 28, 14:35") across all time windows, improving clarity
when hovering over data points. Previously, 6h/24h windows showed only
time, and 30d/all windows showed only date.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: c79ffcdef6ce

---
f95a90e Clean up unknown Claude account entries
Entire-Checkpoint: 4f0fe5f94872

---

content.thinking:
OK je vois les conventions du projet :
- **Conventional commits** : oui, `fix(scope):` ou `refactor(scope):` etc.
- **Langue** : anglais (pas de French commits rĂ©cemment dans les 6 derniers)
- **Style** : courte description du sujet, puis un corps multilignes qui explique le POURQUOI
- **Co-Author** : systĂ©matiquement `Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>` ou `Claude Opus 4.7`
- **Entire-Checkpoint** : chaque commit a aussi une ligne `Entire-Checkpoint:`

Mon changement corrige un crash lors de la suppression d'une chart configuration. C'est un bug fix. Scope: `chart-settings` ou simplement `chart-delete`.

Sujet court (~72 chars max): `fix(chart-delete): resolve binding out-of-bounds crash`

Pourquoi : le binding capturait un index figĂ© qui pouvait devenir invalide pendant une transaction SwiftUI de suppression. FixĂ© en rĂ©solvant par id plutĂ´t que par index.

Je vais stage et committer.

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
fix(chart-settings): resolve binding out-of-bounds crash on delete

The configurationBinding getter was capturing index by value, then
accessing preferences.chartConfigurations[index] in its closure. During
a SwiftUI state transaction for chart deletion, the binding's get was
re-evaluated after removeAll(_:) shrunk the array but before the card
view was removed from the tree â€” causing Array._checkSubscript to trap.

Fixed by having ChartConfigurationCard resolve the configuration by id
rather than capturing index. The binding now re-resolves the index in
its getter/setter via firstIndex(where:), making it immune to
concurrent array mutations.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
